Bedrock University · Overview

        

          Role Tracks
        

        
Each role earns its badge by completing the modules that cover that workflow. Modules add as the platform builds out — you'll never have to "wait until everything is ready" to start.


        

          How this works in practice: open a module, read it, do the practice exercise, then click Mark complete at the bottom. Your role progress updates here automatically. Skip the academic vibe — this is operator training, not coursework.
        


        


        
Why we built this

        
Most HOA management firms train their staff by shadowing a senior manager for a few months and hoping institutional knowledge transfers. It doesn't, consistently — which is why most firms are held hostage by their most experienced people. Bedrock University is the structured alternative: every operator competency is documented as a module, every module ends in a try-it task, every role earns a visible badge.

        
The point isn't credentials. It's that a new hire can become productive in week one because the system itself teaches them how to use it — not by reading a manual, but by doing the work with the module open next to them.


        
A note on iteration

        
The platform is moving fast. If a module mentions a button that isn't where it says it is, or a workflow that's changed since the module was written — flag it to Ed. The modules update as the platform stabilizes; we're shipping training alongside the work rather than waiting for a "finished" state that would never arrive.

      


      
      
      

        
Bedrock University · Day 1 Orientation

        

          Day 1 Orientation
        

        
The full-day onboarding agenda. Same shape for every new hire. Same outcome: by the end of today, they know why we exist, who they're working with, what the platform does, and what their first week looks like.


        

          
          
        


        

          Facilitator mode. This is what you see Monday morning. Each section has what to do, what to say, and what success looks like. Toggle to Participant view above to see what the new hires would see if you projected this on the screen.
        

        

          Welcome. This is your Day 1 at Bedrock. Here's the full day at a glance, and what to expect from each section. Don't worry about absorbing everything today — the goal is orientation, not mastery. Questions are expected and welcomed throughout.
        


        
The day at a glance


        

          9:00 - 9:30
          
The Manifesto Presentation
Why we exist. What we believe. What you're part of.

        

        

          9:30 - 10:00
          
Team Introductions
Meet Martha, Lizette, Celina, Ed.

        

        

          10:00 - 11:00
          
Computer and Systems Setup
Microsoft 365, OneDrive, trustEd bookmarks, email signature.

        

        

          11:00 - 12:00
          
trustEd Platform Tour
Six stops: Ask Ed, DRV, ACC, Portal, Claire/Isabella, Dashboard.

        

        

          12:00 - 1:00
          
Lunch
Phones down. Culture time, not work time.

        

        

          1:00 - 3:00
          
Shadow Real Work
Watch real tasks. Ask questions. No production work today.

        

        

          3:00 - 4:00
          
Wrap and Q&A
What surprised you. What you're excited about. What's next.

        


        
        
        
        

          
9:00 - 9:30
The Manifesto Presentation

          
This is not HR orientation. This is why we exist, what we believe, and what they're part of.


          

            Facilitator

            Walk through the onboarding deck together. Don't rush it. Let the slides breathe. Set the tone for everything that follows — deliver it like you mean it, not like you're reading slides. No laptops open during this section. Just listen and ask questions. End the section on the last slide: "You didn't join an HOA company, friend." That phrase carries everything else.
          

          

            Participant

            The first 30 minutes are about why Bedrock exists, what makes it different, and what role you'll play. Listen, ask questions, don't take notes yet — we want you focused on the ideas, not the logistics. The logistics are easy and come later.
          

          

            By end of section
            
    
              
  • We're building the operating system for community management
    • 
              
  • Your role is the judgment layer, not clerical work
    • 
              
  • Every correction you make trains the system
    • 
              
  • By week six, you'll own one specific thing deeply
    • 
            

          

        


        
        
        
        

          
9:30 - 10:00
Team Introductions

          
No hierarchy theater. Real conversation about how work actually flows.


          

            Facilitator

            Bring everyone in who's around. Have each person say their name, what they do day to day, and one thing the new hires should ask them about in week one. Keep it conversational — this is a moment to set the tone that questions are welcome and people are reachable.
          

          

            Participant

            You'll meet the team and learn what each person does. Don't worry about remembering everything — these are the people you'll work with daily and you'll learn their styles by working together. Ask anything you're curious about.
          

          

            People you'll work with
            
    
              
  • Martha — community manager, day-to-day operations
    • 
              
  • Lizette — field, clubhouse operations
    • 
              
  • Celina — accounting
    • 
              
  • Ed — platform, strategy, everything else
    • 
            

          

        


        
        
        
        

          
10:00 - 11:00
Computer and Systems Setup

          
Get fully operational before anything else. Don't skip steps — a half-configured computer creates friction all week.


          

            Facilitator

            This takes longer than you think. Plan to use the full hour. Sit with each person as they go through the checklist below. Don't move on until every box is checked — if email doesn't send by 11:00, the next sections suffer.
          

          

            Participant

            Work through the checklist below. If anything is missing, malfunctioning, or unclear — say so right away. The point of this hour is to leave with a fully working setup.
          


          
Setup checklist:

          
    
            
  • Microsoft 365 login confirmed
    • 
            
  • Email sending and receiving tested
    • 
            
  • OneDrive synced
    • 
            
  • Chrome profile set up
    • 
            
  • Bookmarks saved: trustEd, Vantaca, SharePoint
    • 
            
  • Phone number and extension confirmed
    • 
            
  • Email signature set up with Bedrock template
    • 
          

        


        
        
        
        

          
11:00 - 12:00
trustEd Platform Tour

          
This is an overview, not training. Understand what exists and how the pieces connect. Deep training happens Days 2 through 5.


          

            Facilitator

            Six stops below. The platform tour can run long — that's okay. Let them ask questions. The goal is curiosity, not speed. For each stop, show the actual surface in trustEd, not slides. Let them watch you (or Martha) do real work in it.
          

          

            Participant

            You'll see six parts of the platform. The point isn't to remember the buttons. The point is to feel what the platform does, where the data lives, and how the pieces talk to each other. Ask anything that's confusing — confusion is a system problem, not a you problem.
          


          

            STOP 1 10 min
            
Ask Ed

            
What it is: The AI knowledge layer. Answers homeowner questions using community-specific governing documents and playbooks.

            
What to show: Type a real homeowner question. Watch it pull the answer. Show how the response gets reviewed before it goes out.

            
Key concept: The AI drafts. You supervise. Every correction makes it smarter.

          


          

            STOP 2 15 min
            
DRV Module

            
What it is: Drive-by inspection workflow. Photos, violation documentation, letter generation, certified mail tracking.

            
What to show: Walk through a completed DRV case end to end. Photo upload, the violation assessment, the letter that gets generated, the audit trail.

            
Key concept: The system does the paperwork. You make the judgment call on whether the violation is real and whether the letter is right.

          


          

            STOP 3 10 min
            
ACC Module

            
What it is: Architectural Control Committee workflow. Homeowner submits an application, system checks completeness, routes for review.

            
What to show: Show Martha using it. Show how fast she can process an application now versus before.

            
Key concept: Martha said "this is so easy." That's the standard for every module we build.

          


          

            STOP 4 10 min
            
Homeowner Portal

            
What it is: Self-service layer for homeowners. Key fob orders, ARC applications, violation status, community information.

            
What to show: Walk through it as a homeowner would. Show what they see when they log in.

            
Key concept: Every feature in the portal reduces inbound calls to the team.

          


          

            STOP 5 10 min
            
Claire and Isabella

            
What it is: AI voice agents answering homeowner calls in English and Spanish.

            
What to show: Play a real call or demo call. Show how Claire handles a routine question and escalates a complex one.

            
Key concept: Claire handles the routine. You handle the human moments that require judgment and empathy.

          


          

            STOP 6 5 min
            
The Dashboard

            
What it is: Portfolio-wide view of community health. AR aging, violation status, ARC activity, response times.

            
What to show: Show what you see as the operator. Show how data from every module feeds into one place.

            
Key concept: Nothing falls through the cracks because everything is visible.

          

        


        
        
        
        

          
12:00 - 1:00
Lunch

          
Culture is built in moments like this, not in training sessions.


          

            Facilitator

            You're buying. Don't skip it. Don't cut it short. Put the phones down. Get to know each other as people. This is not a working lunch.
          

          

            Participant

            Lunch is on Ed. Put your phone away. Get to know the team. This is the easy part.
          

        


        
        
        
        

          
1:00 - 3:00
Shadow Real Work

          
No production work today. Watch and ask questions.


          

            Facilitator

            Pair each new hire with the right shadow assignment based on their role. They watch you or Martha execute real tasks. Default assignments below — adjust based on the morning conversations.
          

          

            Participant

            You're watching. No production work today. Your only job is to ask every question that comes to mind. If something seems inefficient, say so — fresh eyes catch things we've stopped seeing. If something is confusing, that's a system problem, not a you problem.
          


          
Default shadow assignments:

          
    
            
  • Laurie shadows a homeowner communication workflow. Watch Ed or Martha take an inquiry from receipt to response using Ask Ed.
    • 
            
  • Alisha shadows a DRV case from start to finish. Watch how photos get taken, uploaded, assessed, and turned into a letter.
    • 
            
  • Your wife shadows whatever feels most natural based on morning conversations. Likely administrative workflow or homeowner communications.
    • 
          


          

            Rules for shadowing
            
    
              
  • Ask every question that comes to mind. Curiosity gets rewarded here.
    • 
              
  • If something seems inefficient, say so. Fresh eyes catch things we've stopped seeing.
    • 
              
  • If something is confusing, that's a system problem, not a you problem.
    • 
            

          

        


        
        
        
        

          
3:00 - 4:00
Wrap and Q&A

          
Open floor.


          

            Facilitator

            Open floor. Ask: what surprised you, what are you excited about, what are you nervous about, what questions didn't get answered today? Then close with three things:
            
    
              
  1. What success looks like in week one
    2. 
              
  2. How to ask for help (don't struggle silently)
    3. 
              
  3. What "by week six you own one specific thing" actually means for each of them specifically
    4. 
            

          

          

            Participant

            The day ends with an open conversation. Bring whatever questions, observations, or worries came up. Nothing's off-limits.
          

        


        
        
        
        
What you should know by end of Day 1

        
    
          
  • Why Bedrock exists and what makes it different
    • 
          
  • Who everyone is and how the team works
    • 
          
  • Where to find everything you need
    • 
          
  • What the platform does at a high level
    • 
          
  • What your first week looks like
    • 
          
  • That questions are expected and welcomed
    • 
        


        
What you should NOT do on Day 1

        
    
          
  • Send any communication to a homeowner without review
    • 
          
  • Make any changes in Vantaca without guidance
    • 
          
  • Feel like you need to know everything already
    • 
        


        

          
Facilitator notes (Ed)

          
    
            
  • Keep the energy high in the morning. The presentation sets the tone — deliver it like you mean it.
    • 
            
  • Lunch is important. Don't skip it or cut it short.
    • 
            
  • The platform tour can run long. That's okay. Let them ask questions. The goal is curiosity, not speed.
    • 
            
  • End the day on the last slide of the presentation: "You didn't join an HOA company, friend." Remind them what they signed up for.
    • 
          

        


        
      


      
      

        
Module 1 · Foundations

        

          askEd Foundations
        

        
15-20 min read · finish with the practice exercise


        
Why this matters

        
askEd helps you handle homeowner and board communications, look up community-specific facts, and review drafts before they go out. Used well, it lets you respond faster and more consistently — and catches small things you might miss when you're rushed.

        
What matters most is the quality of what we send. Everything askEd produces still passes through you before it reaches a homeowner or a board. This module covers how to use each mode and what to verify before sending.


        
Four modes

        
askEd has four modes, picked by the pills at the top of the askEd tab. The mode changes the framing for what you need — pick the one that matches the task in front of you.


        

          
            

              Mode
              When to use
            

          
          
            
🧠 AdvisorYou don't know what to do. Describe the situation, attach any docs/photos, get full guidance with reasoning.

            
✉️ Draft a ResponseA homeowner emailed; you need to reply. Paste the email, get a ready-to-send draft in Bedrock voice.

            
✏️ Review My DraftYou wrote something; want it checked before sending. Paste your draft, get coaching plus an improved version.

            
⚡ Quick LookupYou just need a fact. Vendor phone, deadline, voting threshold — terse one-or-two-sentence answer.

          
        


        
What "good" looks like — the 5-point check

        
Before you send anything askEd produced, verify these five things. If any fails, edit:

        
    
          
  1. Facts are correct. Names, dates, addresses, contract amounts — check them against the source. The system is usually right but occasionally invents details that sound plausible.
    2. 
          
  2. Tone is warm, not corporate. Bad: "The Association has determined that..." Good: "We've reviewed your request and..."
    3. 
          
  3. Jurisdiction is right. Does this belong with the HOA, or is it actually a city or police matter? Parking on the street, noise after certain hours, abandoned vehicles — many of these are not HOA enforcement. Redirect if not.
    4. 
          
  4. Sign-off is canonical. Always "Bedrock Association Management" — never a personal name in the signature.
    5. 
          
  5. Empathy is present when needed. If the homeowner expressed frustration, the response acknowledges it before answering. Cold replies — even technically correct ones — create the next escalation.
    6. 
        

        

          Rule of thumb: if you're rewriting more than half of what the response said, mention it to Ed — it's the kind of thing he wants to know about.
        


        
When to escalate — never send

        
Push the message to Ed (or whoever is senior on shift) before drafting or sending when any of these surface:

        
    
          
  • Anyone mentions a lawyer, lawsuit, "legal action," or attorney representation
    • 
          
  • Fair Housing Act triggers — disability accommodations (including emotional support animals), family status, religion, race, national origin
    • 
          
  • Threats — to anyone, to property, or implied through tone
    • 
          
  • Vendor accusations of nonpayment or unsafe conditions
    • 
          
  • Money over $5,000 the board hasn't already approved
    • 
          
  • A homeowner accusing Bedrock of negligence, breach of contract, or unethical behavior
    • 
          
  • Anything you feel uncertain about — escalation is never the wrong choice, missed escalation is
    • 
        

        
For these, the platform IS still useful — use Advisor mode to think through how to respond, but route the final response through Ed before sending.


        
Common pitfalls to catch

        
    
          
  • Cold or dismissive tone — add a warm acknowledgment at the top
    • 
          
  • Fabricated rule — AI cited a rule that's not actually in the docs (rare but happens; the 5-point check catches it)
    • 
          
  • Generic "the Board has determined" language — replace with the direct answer
    • 
          
  • Missing courtesy in violation notices — never accusatory ("you are in violation"), always observational ("we wanted to bring this to your attention so it doesn't escalate")
    • 
          
  • Personal name in signature — always Bedrock Association Management
    • 
          
  • Wrong jurisdiction — pointing a homeowner at the HOA for something the city enforces, or vice versa
    • 
        


        
Practice now (do this once)

        
    
          
  1. Open the 🧠 askEd tab.
    2. 
          
  2. Pick any community in the dropdown above.
    3. 
          
  3. Advisor mode: Type "Homeowner is asking about an exception to the pet weight limit because they have an emotional support animal — how do I respond?" Read the guidance. Notice how it handles the Fair Housing dimension. (Also: this is exactly the kind of situation you'd escalate before drafting.)
    4. 
          
  4. Quick Lookup mode: Type "{community name} quorum". See how terse the answer is — that's the right shape for fact-style questions.
    5. 
          
  5. Draft mode: Paste any non-sensitive homeowner email from a recent message. Get a draft. Apply the 5-point check.
    6. 
          
  6. Review mode: Paste a draft you wrote in the past. Get the coached version. Compare to what you originally sent — what would you change?
    7. 
        

        
Time-box this to ~20 minutes. The point isn't to master every mode in one sitting; it's to feel the difference between them and know which one to reach for in real work.


        
If something looks off

        
If askEd gives wrong information, misses something important, or gives you output you have to heavily rewrite — mention it to Ed. A quick email with a screenshot and what you wished the response had said is plenty. He'd rather hear about it than not.


        
Mastery signals

        
You're using askEd well when:

        
    
          
  • You're using all four modes routinely — not just Draft
    • 
          
  • You're applying the 5-point check on every response before it goes out
    • 
          
  • You're catching things in Review mode before sending that would have been embarrassments
    • 
          
  • You're flagging legal/Fair Housing situations to Ed before drafting, not after
    • 
          
  • You feel comfortable using it on most homeowner emails without needing to ask
    • 
        


        

          Questions or anything unclear — email Ed.
        


        

          

            
Finished this module?

            
Marking complete updates your role progress on the Overview page.

          

          

            ✓ Completed
            
            
          

        

      


      
      

        
Module 2 · Inspector Track

        

          Field Inspection Capture
        

        
20-25 min · finish with one real inspection walk


        
Why this matters

        
Field inspections are how Bedrock documents what's actually happening at a community. Done well, they create a defensible audit trail — every photo timestamped, GPS-tagged, AI-categorized, and linked to the right property. Done poorly, they're a stack of phone pictures nobody can sort through later.

        
The consistency matters as much as the photo itself. A community that gets walked once a month produces a fair-housing record we can stand behind: "every property was inspected, every violation was opened on the same standard, every letter went out in the same window." That's the structural advantage. Your job during the walk is to make the system's job easy.


        
What happens behind the scenes

        
Every photo you capture flows through this pipeline — automatically, in the background, while you keep walking:

        
    
          
  1. Your phone tags it with GPS coordinates, compass heading, and capture time.
    2. 
          
  2. The system snaps it to the right property — the GPS + heading lets us figure out which house you were facing.
    3. 
          
  3. Bedrock AI looks at the photo and decides: is there a deed-restriction issue here? Which category? How severe?
    4. 
          
  4. A violation letter gets drafted and dropped into the Drafts queue, in the right tone, citing the right governing-doc section.
    5. 
          
  5. You review the drafts at the end of the day, approve the ones that are right, reject the ones that aren't.
    6. 
        

        
This whole flow is invisible during the walk. You're not waiting for analysis — you're capturing.


        
Before you start a walk

        
Open the Inspect tab on your phone. Three things to check before tapping Start session:

        
    
          
  • Right community is selected. The dropdown at the top is what scopes everything that follows. Wrong community = photos snap to the wrong property database.
    • 
          
  • Community boundary is loaded. If you see "no boundary set" in red, the system can't tell which properties are inside scope. Flag to Ed before you start.
    • 
          
  • Phone has location permission. If iOS is asking every time, allow "While Using." Without it, photos can't snap to properties.
    • 
        


        
Three modes — pick the right one

        
The mode determines how aggressively the system snaps photos to properties and how it expects the data to look. Pick wrong and you create cleanup work for yourself later.


        

          
            

              Mode
              When to use
            

          
          
            
🚗 Drive-byYou're driving the streets. The system expects single-photo-per-house, rapid pace, may capture neighbors' houses partially. Most-forgiving mode.

            
🚶 Walk-throughYou're on foot through a community or common areas. Slower, multiple photos per property. Use this for thorough monthly inspections.

            
📹 Mounted cameraPhone is mounted on the dash, capturing continuously while driving. Use only for batch-image dumps you'll review after the drive. Highest false-positive rate — the system flags more for human review.

            
🔍 Resale walkYou're inspecting one specific property because of a resale request. System knows you're not doing the whole community — single-property scope.

          
        


        
The wide + close-up pattern

        
Every property you capture gets two kinds of shots, in this order:

        
    
          
  1. 🏠 Wide shot first — frames the whole front of the house, ideally with the address visible (number on the curb, mailbox, plaque). This is the wrong-house insurance. If a homeowner ever challenges the citation, the wide shot proves which property we documented.
    2. 
          
  2. 🔍 Close-up(s) of the issue — one for each concern. Overgrown grass, faded paint, unapproved structure. The system pairs every close-up to the wide automatically — the chip above the capture button shows what you're paired with.
    3. 
        

        
For a property with three issues, the right pattern is 1 wide + 3 close-ups, all paired together. When you're done at that property, tap "Done with property" to start a fresh pair for the next house.


        

          Why we do it this way: the wide shot lands on every violation letter alongside the close-up. When a homeowner calls saying "that's not my house," the wide shot ends the conversation in 30 seconds — there's the address number, there's their door, there's the issue. The single most defensible inspection record we can produce.
        


        
How to capture well

        
    
          
  • Stand still when you tap Capture. The GPS+heading lock is sharpest for the half-second the photo is taken. Walking-and-snapping degrades the property snap.
    • 
          
  • Wide first, every time. Default behavior — first tap on a new property captures the wide. Resist the temptation to skip it for "obvious" houses. The wide shot is for the conversation 6 weeks from now, not this moment.
    • 
          
  • Frame the close-up tight on the issue. The system reads close-ups better than wide-zoom shots. Overgrown grass → frame the section that's tall. Faded paint → frame a panel of the faded area.
    • 
          
  • Multiple issues = multiple close-ups, one wide. Tap "Capture close-up" once per concern. They all pair to the same wide. Don't restart the pair between issues at the same property.
    • 
          
  • Don't double-capture. If you took a photo and aren't sure it landed, scroll down — you'll see it in the photo strip within ~3 seconds. Re-tapping creates duplicate observations.
    • 
          
  • "Single shot" escape hatch. If you genuinely need just one photo (e.g., a quick re-check of one issue you already documented), tap the small Single shot link below the capture button. Skips the pair.
    • 
          
  • Skip the obvious-not-a-violation shots. A perfectly-maintained yard doesn't need a wide+close-up. If you wouldn't write a letter about it, don't capture it.
    • 
        


        

          Rule of thumb: the photo is the evidence. If someone challenges the violation six weeks later, will this photo (timestamped, GPS-tagged, with the issue clearly visible) be enough? If not, take a better one now while you're standing there.
        


        
After the walk — Drafts queue review

        
Back at the office (or on your phone over coffee), open the Drafts queue in the Inspect tab. Each photo the system categorized as a likely violation has a draft letter waiting. Your job:

        
    
          
  1. Look at the photo. Does it show what the draft says it shows? If the system wrote "yard not maintained" but the photo is actually a parked trailer — that's a miscategorization.
    2. 
          
  2. Verify the address. The "wrong house" check — does the property the system snapped to match the property in the photo? A house number visible in the shot is the gold-standard tiebreaker.
    3. 
          
  3. Read the draft letter. Apply the 5-point check from Module 1 (askEd Foundations) — facts, tone, jurisdiction, sign-off, empathy.
    4. 
          
  4. Approve in bulk or one-by-one. Select all the ones that look good, click Approve. The rejected ones go back for correction or get voided.
    5. 
        


        
Quality status — Verified vs. Corrected

        
Every violation has a quality status that signals how trustworthy the record is for later analysis. As an inspector, you control two of them:

        
    
          
  • ✓ Verified — you reviewed the photo, the categorization is right, the address is right. Full weight on this record going forward. Use liberally — most are verified.
    • 
          
  • ⚠ Correct — something's wrong. Maybe the photo got snapped to the neighbor's house, maybe the system called it the wrong category, maybe the homeowner already fixed it before the letter would have gone out. Correcting voids the record and (if applicable) opens a fresh one against the right data.
    • 
        

        
Don't agonize. If you're 80% confident, mark Verified. If you're not, correct it — the audit trail is cleaner if "uncertainty" is captured at correction time, not buried in your memory.


        
When to escalate — pause before approving

        
Some categories of inspection findings need Ed's eyes before they become letters. Push to him rather than approving:

        
    
          
  • Structural / safety issues (collapsing fence, sagging roof, exposed electrical) — these are bigger than a deed-restriction letter
    • 
          
  • Anything involving a visible disability accommodation (ramp, support animal evidence) — Fair Housing dimension
    • 
          
  • Repeated offender already at certified §209 stage — fine assessments need a moment of thought
    • 
          
  • A property you suspect is a rental — verify residency type before the letter goes to a tenant who can't act on it
    • 
          
  • Anything where you feel "this could blow up" — escalation is never the wrong choice
    • 
        


        
Common pitfalls

        
    
          
  • Mode mismatch — driving with Walk-through selected, or mounting the phone in Drive-by mode. Check the mode pill at the top before you start moving.
    • 
          
  • Capturing the same yard from three angles — pick the best frame and move on; duplicates create duplicate violations.
    • 
          
  • Approving without looking at the photo — Drafts queue is fast, but the photo is the evidence. Glance at every one.
    • 
          
  • Inspecting on a day after a storm or right after lawn-mowing day — context matters. A blown-down trash can isn't a violation; the homeowner didn't choose that.
    • 
          
  • Skipping the wrong-house check — the highest-cost error is mailing a violation to the wrong address. Two minutes of address-verification is cheaper than one apology call.
    • 
        


        
Practice now (do this once, with a real community)

        
    
          
  1. Open the Inspect tab. Pick a community where Bedrock manages a real book.
    2. 
          
  2. Verify the community has a boundary loaded (top of the panel). If not, stop and ask Ed.
    3. 
          
  3. Tap Start session, pick Walk-through mode.
    4. 
          
  4. Walk one short street or one common area. Capture 5-10 photos: half should be actual concerns, half should be "this looks fine" (to test what the system filters out).
    5. 
          
  5. End the session. Wait ~5 minutes for the categorization to finish.
    6. 
          
  6. Open the Drafts queue below. Review every draft against its photo. Approve the right ones, correct the wrong ones.
    7. 
          
  7. Open the Cure-lapse processor panel and tap Preview — see how the violations you just opened will eventually move through the stages without you doing anything.
    8. 
        

        
Time-box this to ~25 minutes. The point isn't to perfect the workflow on day one; it's to feel the rhythm: capture → AI does its work → you review → letters land in the mail queue. Once that loop feels natural, the rest is volume.


        
If something looks off

        
If the system consistently mis-categorizes something (e.g. mistakes mulch for unmaintained yard), the photo snapped to the wrong house, or a draft letter quotes a governing doc section that isn't right — flag it to Ed with a screenshot. Pattern errors are how the platform gets better; one-off corrections only fix that one violation.


        
Mastery signals

        
You're inspecting well when:

        
    
          
  • You can do a full walk-through of a 200-property community in under 2 hours without capturing junk
    • 
          
  • Your Drafts queue review takes <30 seconds per draft because the photos and categorizations are clean
    • 
          
  • You catch wrong-house snaps before approving — every time
    • 
          
  • You're using Correct, not delete, when something's wrong — the audit trail tells the right story
    • 
          
  • You're seeing patterns the system misses ("this category seems off for stucco-style houses") and reporting them upstream
    • 
        


        

          Questions about field work or anything unclear — email Ed.
        


        

          

            
Finished this module?

            
Mark complete once you've done at least one real walk-through.

          

          

            ✓ Completed
            
            
          

        

      


      

      
      

        
Module 3 · DRV Operator Track

        

          Drafts Queue Review
        

        

          

            🚧
            
Under construction

          

          
This module isn't written yet. The underlying workflow (Drafts queue, approve/reject flow, mail-merge to PDF) is still iterating — Phase 6c-6e shipped in the last two weeks, with quality + corrections layered on top this week. Writing the module now would lock us into content that gets stale by month-end.

          
For now: shadow Ed on a Drafts queue review session, or open the Inspect tab → Drafts queue and try it on real drafts. The workflow is intuitive; the module will document the edge cases once we know what they are.

        

        
What this module will cover

        
    
          
  • The Drafts queue lifecycle — from AI-categorized photo to mail-ready PDF
    • 
          
  • Per-photo review etiquette — what to look at first, what's safe to skim
    • 
          
  • Bulk approve vs. one-by-one — when each is right
    • 
          
  • Reject vs. correct vs. void — quality-status decisions
    • 
          
  • Mail Queue handoff — batch PDF generation, delivery method splits
    • 
          
  • Common failure modes — wrong-house snaps, AI category drift, photo blur
    • 
        

        

          Want this prioritized? Mention it to Ed — module order follows what's actually slowing operators down.
        

      


      
      

        
Module 4 · DRV Operator Track

        

          Cure-Lapse Handling
        

        

          

            🚧
            
Under construction

          

          
The daily cure-lapse processor just shipped (auto-bumps violations past their cure date, fires at 06:00 Central). It needs a week of real-world runs before we know which edge cases to document — does the board-review flag fire correctly when fines are paused? Do certified §209 lapses behave on weekends? Writing the module today is premature.

          
For now: check the "Last automatic run" indicator on the Inspect tab → Cure-lapse panel daily for the next two weeks. Flag anything weird to Ed.

        

        
What this module will cover

        
    
          
  • How the cure-lapse processor decides what to escalate
    • 
          
  • The four-stage ladder: courtesy 1 → courtesy 2 → certified §209 → fine assessed
    • 
          
  • The board-review flag — when fines are paused, what the operator does next
    • 
          
  • Manual triggers vs. waiting for the daily fire
    • 
          
  • Edge cases: holiday cures, disputed violations, residency-type mismatches
    • 
        

        

          Want this prioritized? Mention it to Ed.
        

      


      
      

        
Module 5 · DRV Operator Track

        

          Fine Assessment & Schedules
        

        

          

            🚧
            
Under construction

          

          
Per-category fine schedules + the Fine Queue shipped last week (Bundle 3). The default-off master toggle is intentional — most boards haven't signed off on fine amounts yet. Until the schedules are populated for each community and the board has approved them, this module would teach a workflow that nobody is running.

          
For now: do not assess fines without explicit board approval per community. The system won't auto-fire until the community master toggle is on AND the category schedule is filled in.

        

        
What this module will cover

        
    
          
  • Reading the fine schedule UI — community master + per-category overrides
    • 
          
  • First / second / third / recurring offense amounts — when each applies
    • 
          
  • Board-approval prerequisites — what we need before any fine fires
    • 
          
  • Working the Fine Queue — review, hold, post-to-ledger
    • 
          
  • Texas §209 prerequisites — when certified §209 must precede a fine
    • 
        

        

          Want this prioritized? Mention it to Ed.
        

      


      
      

        
Module 6 · Email Triage Track

        

          Email Intake Basics
        

        

          

            🚧
            
Under construction

          

          
The Email Intelligence intake module is stable but the workflow for what to do with extracted facts vs. decisions vs. action items is still being refined. We're also adding owner-reply attachment, near-duplicate detection, and recap generation in parallel. The module gets written once the intake → review → promote → recap loop has felt right for a few weeks.

          
For now: paste any homeowner thread into Email Intelligence → review the extraction → approve to library. Don't over-think dedup or supersession; the system handles those.

        

        
What this module will cover

        
    
          
  • When to paste an email into intake (and when to skip it)
    • 
          
  • Reading the extraction — facts, decisions, action items, vendor mentions
    • 
          
  • Approve to library vs. discard — what reaches askEd vs. what stays internal
    • 
          
  • Recap generation — picking the right window + audience
    • 
          
  • Near-duplicate handling — superseded threads, force-add workflow
    • 
        

        

          Want this prioritized? Mention it to Ed.
        

      


      
      

        
Module 8 · Foundations

        

          Homeowner Advisory Panel
        

        

          

            🚧
            
Under construction

          

          
The Homeowner Advisory Panel is a Bedrock institution we're launching in the next quarter. 10 homeowners across the portfolio — deliberately weighted toward vocal critics — meet quarterly for structured service feedback. This module gets written once we've run the first cycle and know what operators actually need to know about it.

          
For now: every operator should know we run this panel and what it's for. If a homeowner asks about it, the short answer is: "Bedrock convenes a panel of homeowners across our communities each cycle to tell us where we're falling short. It's a deliberate way to hear from people who've been critical of how we work."

        


        
What this is — and what it isn't

        
The Advisory Panel exists for one reason: to make sure Bedrock hears from homeowners we'd otherwise only see complain on Facebook or at meetings. Most management firms run satisfaction surveys nobody fills out. We run a deliberate, structured, small-group panel where the loudest critics get a seat at the table — and a charter that keeps the conversation productive.

        
It is: a service-feedback channel. The panel tells Bedrock how we should deliver our work — letter quality, response times, enforcement consistency, communication, what's broken about how we operate.

        
It is not: a policy body. Panel discussions don't decide ACC standards, fine schedules, or rule changes — that's the board's role at each community. We're crystal-clear about this in every session.


        
Why this exists

        
When a homeowner is angry about how we managed a violation, an amenity, or a board communication, two things happen: they tell other homeowners, and they don't tell us — at least not in any form we can act on. The panel reverses that. Instead of arguing on Facebook, we invite the critic into a room where we have to listen and they have to be specific. The result is feedback we can actually use, and a measurable shift in how those critics describe Bedrock to their neighbors.

        
It's also a franchise-grade story competitors can't match. Spectrum, Associa, and the large regionals send Net Promoter Score emails. Bedrock has a Homeowner Advisory Panel.


        
Panel composition (10 seats)

        
    
          
  • 5 vocal critics — homeowners who've been publicly or directly critical of how Bedrock works. Sourced by name from Facebook screenshots, board meetings, formal complaints, and community-manager nominations.
    • 
          
  • 3 constructive engaged homeowners — not necessarily satisfied, but pragmatic; they model productive conversation.
    • 
          
  • 2 long-tenure historians — 5+ years in their community; they provide context and steady the room when discussions get reactive.
    • 
        

        
Term is six months with optional re-up. Every community in our portfolio gets at least one seat where possible.


        
The charter — what panelists agree to

        
    
          
  1. Current on dues; no active legal action against Bedrock or their HOA.
    2. 
          
  2. Chatham House Rule — what's said in the room stays in the room. Themes are shareable; attribution is not.
    3. 
          
  3. Problem + solution format. No pure venting. Every grievance includes "what would good look like."
    4. 
          
  4. Service feedback, not policy. Policy concerns are relayed to the relevant board separately.
    5. 
          
  5. No using your panel seat to lobby your own board or community.
    6. 
          
  6. The panel addresses Bedrock's portfolio-wide service delivery, not your individual situation — those go through normal channels and remain effective in their own venue.
    7. 
        

        
Violations = removal, no re-recycle next cycle.


        
What this module will cover (once written)

        
    
          
  • How operators talk about the panel when a homeowner asks
    • 
          
  • How to recognize and route panel-relevant feedback that surfaces in everyday operations
    • 
          
  • What changes after a panel cycle — how the feedback shows up in service delivery
    • 
          
  • How to keep the panel and the board roles cleanly separated
    • 
          
  • When a panel member's concern needs escalation to Ed before the next session
    • 
        

        

          First panel cycle runs after the v1 platform stabilizes. Module ships after cycle 1 wraps and we know what worked.
        

      


      
      

        
Module 7 · Email Triage Track

        

          Owner Reply → Violation
        

        

          

            🚧
            
Under construction

          

          
This workflow shipped today — when an intake might be about an open violation, the system suggests candidate violations and the operator attaches with one click. Needs a few weeks of real owner replies before we know what the operator actually trips on (false matches? missed matches? attaching to the wrong household?).

          
For now: open any Email Intelligence intake → scroll past the extraction blocks. If the dashed "Owner reply on a violation?" panel suggests a match, eyeball the address + score reasons before clicking Attach.

        

        
What this module will cover

        
    
          
  • How the candidate-matching scorer works (address signals, sender match, recency)
    • 
          
  • When to trust the top match vs. when to scroll the full list
    • 
          
  • What happens when you attach — interaction row on the timeline, contact history, audit trail
    • 
          
  • Detaching mistakes — when and how (it's safe; the intake is untouched)
    • 
          
  • When a reply needs Ed before any response goes out (legal threats, ADA, threats)
    • 
        

        

          Want this prioritized? Mention it to Ed.
